revisionLog.whatChanged: A project-owner scientific/linguistic review of rev-003's explanatory prose (recorded via ch01-english-baseline-rev-003) identified several inaccuracies and revised the wording accordingly: (1) distance/time/mass/charge are now explicitly scoped as 'the quantities this chapter's introductory framework builds its early examples from', not a universal list of every fundamental quantity in physics; (2) the claim that 'every physical quantity...ultimately traces back to' those four was replaced with a context-limited statement; (3) added a new closing 'Scientific note' paragraph stating that in the modern SI system, electric current (not charge) is one of the seven base quantities and charge is derived (Q = I t); (4) 'speed...without needing any unit of its own' was replaced with an explicit statement that speed's derived unit is m/s, formed from distance's and time's own units; (5) 'Saying "far" is not physics; saying "100 m" is' was replaced with a qualitative-vs-quantitative framing; (6) the area/volume/density mentions were trimmed to a brief one-sentence mention (deferred to later sections) rather than developed in this opening slide. The verbatim source-slide quotation at the start of the block is byte-for-byte unchanged from rev-003.
revisionLog.whatWasNotChanged: The verbatim quoted original English slide text ('In physics, there are three basic aspects...distance, time, and two properties of matter called mass and charge.') is unchanged. No record in ch01-t04, and no record in any of the four originally-piloted topics, was changed.
revisionLog.rationale: Project-owner scientific and linguistic review of the draft-ai-generated-unreviewed content added in rev-003 found it presented a textbook-introductory simplification (charge as a 'fundamental' quantity alongside distance/time/mass) as if it were the complete, universal SI base-quantity list, and contained two overstated/imprecise sentences (the 'no unit of its own' claim for speed, and the 'far is not physics' framing). This revision corrects the wording and adds an explicit SI scientific note, without altering the preserved verbatim slide quotation.

revisionLog.whatChanged: Project-owner pedagogical review of rev-004's wording (now scientifically accurate, per rev-004) found the explanatory prose too long and repetitive for an introductory slide. Restructured into a compact instructional format — Main Idea, five short numbered conceptual steps, Simple Example, Common Misconception, Scientific Note, Connection to the Next Part — replacing the prior sequence of long, loosely-ordered paragraphs. Removed the duplicated explanation of speed's derived unit (m/s, formed from distance's and time's own units), which previously appeared once before the worked example and again after it; the idea is now stated once (in the numbered steps) and the post-example sentence is a short, generic closing ('This example shows how a derived quantity is formed by combining two measured quantities.'). Replaced 'treats distance, time, mass, and charge as the quantities used to describe them in this context' with 'introduces distance, time, mass, and charge as important measurable quantities within its introductory framework'. Replaced 'Distance is the measurable aspect of space.' with 'Distance is one measurable way of describing separation within space.' No scientific idea introduced in rev-004 (the SI base-quantity note, Q = I t, speed's derived unit) was removed — only shortened and de-duplicated.
revisionLog.whatWasNotChanged: The verbatim quoted original English slide text is unchanged. No instructional prose, equation, visual reference, misconception, or review question in any of ch01-t01's seven pre-existing records was touched. ch01-t01-visual-001.svg was not touched. No record in ch01-t04, and no record in any of the four originally-piloted topics, was changed. All scientific corrections from rev-004 (SI base-quantity accuracy, Q = I t, speed's derived unit m/s) are preserved, just stated once instead of twice and more concisely.
revisionLog.rationale: Project-owner pedagogical review found the rev-004 wording, while scientifically accurate, read as too dense and repetitive for a short opening slide, risking making the chapter's first concept feel unnecessarily heavy for a beginning student. This revision compacts the structure and removes the one clear duplication (the derived-unit-of-speed explanation) without cutting any scientific content.

revisionLog.scope: Four corrections to ch01-t01-block-opening-2 (Slide 2) and one structural/architecture change applied to both ch01-t01-block-opening (Slide 1) and ch01-t01-block-opening-2 (Slide 2) in docs/content-design/chapter-01/batch1-drafts/ch01-t01-content.json. No instructional prose in ch01-t01-block-opening (Slide 1) was changed — only its blockType/slide-metadata tagging (see 'Architecture change' below). No record in ch01-t04 was touched.
revisionLog.whatChanged: (1) Scientific correction — length vs. distance: in Slide 2's Step 1, 'L represents the dimension of length or distance.' was replaced with 'L represents the dimension of length. Distance is one physical quantity that has the dimension of length.', clarifying that length is the base dimension and distance is a quantity that has that dimension. The Main Idea sentence was revised from 'combining three fundamental quantities: distance, mass, and time' to 'using the three base dimensions of length, mass, and time.' The Key Concept sentence's 'Distance, mass, and time form the dimensional foundation...' was revised to 'Length, mass, and time form the dimensional foundation...' for the same reason. The original quoted slide text ('Distance (L)', 'For example, speed has the unit of miles per hour (L/T).') was NOT altered. (2) Governance correction — missingVisual removed: 'missingVisual' was removed from ch01-t01-block-opening-2's blocking.blockingReason. The Figure Explanation subsection was intentionally omitted by explicit project-owner decision because the figure is illustrative and not required for understanding Slide 2's concept — not because the image remains an unresolved gap. No provisional visual record was created; provenanceLinks[0].evidence now documents this explicit decision. All other blocking reasons (translationPending, scientificReviewPending, other) and all publication flags are unchanged. (3) Architecture change — reusable slide collection: the blockType of both ch01-t01-block-opening and ch01-t01-block-opening-2 was changed from the per-slide values 'openingConcept'/'openingConceptSlide2' to one generic, reusable 'slide' blockType, and each record gained slideNumber (1 and 2 respectively) and slideTitleEn fields recording its display order and bilingual title. This is a schema/tagging change only — no character of either slide's approved instructional text was altered by it, and both blockIds (ch01-t01-block-opening, ch01-t01-block-opening-2) are unchanged. The application's normalized model now exposes topic.slides as an ordered array (see src/types/normalized.ts's NormalizedSlide / src/content/adapter.ts's normalizeSlides), so a future Slide 3 requires only a new 'slide'-typed record with the next slideNumber — no new ContentBlockType, no new NormalizedTopic field, no adapter or TopicPage wiring specific to that slide.
revisionLog.whatWasNotChanged: No word of Slide 1's approved instructional prose was reworded, shortened, reordered, or removed — only its blockType tag and new slideNumber/slideTitleEn metadata fields were added, alongside its existing, unchanged blockId and text. Slide 2's original verbatim quotation, all five step titles, the Simple Example calculation, the Common Misconception's core claim, the Scientific Note's mechanics-only scope, and the Connection sentence are otherwise unchanged. ch01-t01-visual-001.svg was not touched. No record in ch01-t04, and no record in any of the four originally-piloted topics, was changed.
revisionLog.rationale: Project-owner scientific, governance, and architecture review of the initial Slide 2 addition (rev-007) identified: a conflation of the length dimension with the distance quantity in the newly authored explanation; an unnecessary 'missingVisual' blocking reason for a figure the project owner decided is not instructionally required; and a schema design (one new ContentBlockType and one new NormalizedTopic field per slide) that would not scale to a third slide without further schema changes. This revision applies the project owner's exact corrected wording, removes the unneeded blocking reason with its rationale documented in provenance, and replaces the per-slide schema with a generic, reusable slide collection.

revisionLog.whatChanged: (1) The Simple Example's unit-consistency sentence was revised from 'Before comparing or combining them, their units must be made consistent: 2 m = 200 cm.' to 'Before comparing these measurements or using them together in a calculation, their units should be made consistent: 2 m = 200 cm.' (2) The bare calculation paragraph '200 cm × 80 cm × 75 cm' was relabeled 'Dimensions: 200 cm × 80 cm × 75 cm' to avoid implying an unlabeled multiplication result, and a new clarifying sentence was added immediately after it: 'The multiplication signs separate the three dimensions here; no volume calculation is required in this example.' (3) The Scientific Note was refined from 'Millimeter, centimeter, and kilometer are metric multiples or submultiples of the meter.' to the more precise 'The millimeter and centimeter are decimal submultiples of the meter, while the kilometer is a decimal multiple of it.'

The verbatim original English quotation, Main Idea, Steps 1-4, the Common Misconception, the Scientific Note, the Key Concept, and the Connection to the Next Slide are unchanged. No record in ch01-t01 other than Slide 7, and no record in ch01-t04, was touched.
revisionLog.whatChanged: (1) Step 5's rounding explanation was corrected: the prior sentence 'Rounded to three significant figures, this becomes 23 m ≈ 75.5 ft, a practical, easy-to-read approximate value.' incorrectly presented 75.5 ft (three significant figures) as the answer rounded to three significant figures, when 23 m itself carries only two significant figures. It was replaced with 'The calculated value is 75.45932 ft. Rounded to one decimal place, it is 75.5 ft. However, because the original measurement 23 m is written with two significant figures, the result based strictly on significant figures is approximately 75 ft.', followed by two explicitly labeled results: '23 m ≈ 75.5 ft (rounded to one decimal place)' and '23 m ≈ 75 ft (rounded to two significant figures)'. The unrounded calculation '23 × 3.28084 = 75.45932' and '23 m = 75.45932 ft' are unchanged. (2) The Simple Example was revised from 'Convert 5 m to feet.' / '5 m × (3.28084 ft / 1 m) = 16.4042 ft' / '5 m ≈ 16.4 ft' to 'Convert 5.00 m to feet.' / '5.00 m × (3.28084 ft / 1 m) = 16.4042 ft' / '5.00 m ≈ 16.4 ft', with a new closing sentence 'This result is rounded to three significant figures, matching the three significant figures in 5.00 m.' added to make the number of significant figures in the given measurement and its rounded result explicitly consistent (5.00 m, three significant figures, correctly pairs with a three-significant-figure result, unlike the original Step 5's mismatch). (3) The Conversion-Factor Explanation was revised from 'A conversion factor is a ratio equal to 1 that relates two different units of the same physical quantity. Because 1 ft = 0.3048 m exactly, dividing both sides by 0.3048 m gives 1 = 3.28084 ft / 1 m. ...' to 'A conversion factor is formed from two equivalent measurements. The exact relation is 1 ft = 0.3048 m. Its reciprocal is approximately 1 m = 3.28084 ft, so the displayed conversion factor represents unity to the precision shown and changes only the unit representation.', removing the prior wording's implication that the rounded decimal ratio (3.28084 ft / 1 m) is itself an exact, derived-by-division equality rather than a rounded approximation of the exact relation's reciprocal. src/features/topics/StructuredSlideContent.tsx's ch01-t01-block-opening-7 config entry's equationBlockPhrase second string was updated from '5 m × (3.28084 ft / 1 m) = 16.4042 ft' to '5.00 m × (3.28084 ft / 1 m) = 16.4042 ft' to match the revised Simple Example text, so the Simple Example's equation still renders with the same distinct equation-block styling.
revisionLog.whatWasNotChanged: The verbatim quoted original English slide text ('1.1 Fundamental Physical Quantities: Converting Distance Measurements... Example: Convert 23 meters to feet.'), the Main Idea, Steps 1-4 (including Step 3's '23 m × (3.28084 ft / 1 m)' equation block and Step 4's unit-cancellation explanation), the Common Misconception (including its own '23 m × (3.28084 ft / 1 m)' line), the Scientific Note ('1 in = 2.54 cm exactly', '1 ft = 0.3048 m exactly', and '1 m ≈ 3.28084 ft' as the rounded reciprocal — all three preserved verbatim per explicit project-owner instruction), the Key Concept, and the Connection to the Next Slide are unchanged. Slide 7's nine-section structure, the conversion factor's unit orientation (meters in the denominator, feet in the numerator), the accordion's behavior, Slides 1-6, and every governance/publication flag are unchanged.
revisionLog.rationale: Project-owner scientific-precision review of Slide 7 (rev-014) found that presenting '75.5 ft' as simultaneously 'rounded to three significant figures' was mathematically inconsistent with the original measurement's own two significant figures (23 m), and that describing the rounded conversion factor '3.28084 ft / 1 m' via a 'dividing both sides' derivation implied it was exact rather than a rounded approximation of the exact relation's reciprocal. This revision corrects both statements with precise, explicitly labeled rounding results and a conversion-factor explanation that accurately distinguishes the exact defining relation from its rounded reciprocal, and makes the Simple Example's given/result significant-figure counts explicitly consistent (5.00 m, three significant figures, paired with a three-significant-figure result) to avoid repeating the same category of error there.
